metadata.title: Pharmacokinetics in Patients with Impaired Renal Function – Study Design, Data Analysis, and Impact on Dosing
metadata.note: PDF filename references '2020' but document cover page states March 2024 (Clinical Pharmacology). Rules extracted from the March 2024 version as presented.

rules.rule_text: It is important to characterize a drug's PK in patients with renal impairment to provide appropriate dosage recommendations; the effect of impaired renal function on a drug's PK must be characterized early in drug development so that patients with impaired renal function can be included and appropriately dosed in late-phase clinical studies.
rules.source_section: Section II – Background / Section III – Considerations for Renal Impairment Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: As a result of the above considerations, it is important to characterize a drug's PK in patients with renal impairment to provide appropriate dosage recommendations... FDA recommends that the effect of impaired renal function on a drug's PK be characterized early in drug development so that patients with impaired renal function can be included (and appropriately dosed) in the late-phase clinical studies.
rules.exceptions: Exceptions described in Section III.B for drugs where impaired renal function is not likely to alter PK to a clinically significant degree

rules.rule_text: A dedicated renal impairment study (full pharmacokinetic study design) is recommended when a drug or its active metabolites are substantially eliminated by the kidneys, defined as a fraction of systemically available drug or active metabolite eliminated unchanged in the urine of 0.3 or greater.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1 – Drugs Excreted Primarily by the Kidneys
rules.verbatim_quote: A dedicated renal impairment study (described in section IV.B. Full Pharmacokinetic Study Design) is recommended when impaired renal function is likely to alter the PK of the drug or its active metabolites because they are substantially eliminated by the kidneys. A drug is considered to be substantially eliminated by the kidneys when the fraction of systemically available drug or active metabolite that is eliminated unchanged in the urine is 0.3 or greater.

rules.rule_text: For drugs predominantly eliminated via non-renal (e.g., hepatic) routes, a reduced pharmacokinetic study design must be considered to evaluate the potential impact of severe renal impairment on PK.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.2 – Drugs Excreted Primarily by Other Routes
rules.verbatim_quote: An impact of severe renal impairment on the PK of drugs predominantly eliminated via the hepatic route cannot always be excluded. In such situations, a reduced pharmacokinetic study design (described in section IV.C. Reduced Pharmacokinetic Study Design) should be considered.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacokinetic studies in participants with impaired renal function are recommended for therapeutic proteins and peptides with a molecular weight less than 69 kDa, unless the fraction of systemically available drug or active metabolite eliminated unchanged in the urine is less than 0.3.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.3 – Certain Therapeutic Proteins and Peptides
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, pharmacokinetic studies in participants with impaired renal function are recommended for therapeutic proteins and peptides with a molecular weight less than 69 kDa, unless the fraction of systemically available drug or active metabolite that is eliminated unchanged in the urine is less than 0.3.
rules.exceptions: Proteins/peptides with MW >= 69 kDa generally do not require dedicated renal impairment studies

rules.rule_text: The PK of drugs likely to be used in patients receiving renal replacement therapies must be evaluated to determine the contribution of the specific replacement modality to elimination of the drug and its potentially active metabolites.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.4 – Evaluating the Influence of Renal Replacement Therapies
rules.verbatim_quote: The PK of drugs that are likely to be used in patients receiving renal replacement therapies should be evaluated to determine the contribution of the specific replacement modality (e.g., intermittent or continuous) to the elimination of the drug and its potentially active metabolites.
rules.exceptions: May not be needed if the drug has molecular weight making it unlikely to be cleared by the modality, or if it exhibits high plasma protein binding unaffected by renal impairment

rules.rule_text: A dedicated renal impairment study may not be warranted for gaseous or volatile drugs eliminated through the lungs, drugs intended only for single administration, therapeutic proteins with a molecular weight greater than 69 kDa, and locally acting drugs with limited or no systemic exposure.
rules.source_section: Section III.B – Circumstances Where a Dedicated Study May Not Be Warranted
rules.verbatim_quote: The following are examples where the PK are not expected to be markedly different between patients with normal or impaired renal function. In such cases, a dedicated study may not be warranted: Gaseous or volatile drugs and active metabolites that are primarily eliminated through the lungs; Drugs intended only for single administration; Therapeutic proteins with a molecular weight greater than 69 kDa; Locally acting drugs with limited or no systemic exposure.

rules.rule_text: If a sponsor believes patients with renal impairment should be excluded from clinical studies, the sponsor must include a rationale for this exclusion in the protocol and discuss this exclusion with the FDA.
rules.source_section: Section III – Considerations for Renal Impairment Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: If a sponsor believes that such patients should be excluded from these studies, they should include a rationale for this exclusion in the protocol and discuss this exclusion with the FDA.

rules.rule_text: Renal function classification for dedicated renal impairment studies must use Table 1 categories: Control (normal, GFR >= 90 mL/min), Mild impairment (60 < 90), Moderate impairment (30 < 60), and Severe impairment/kidney failure not receiving dialysis (< 30 mL/min).
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1 – Study Participants
rules.verbatim_quote: The classification of renal function described in Table 1 can be used to enroll participants into the dedicated renal impairment study. [Table 1: Control (normal renal function): >= 90; Mild impairment: 60 < 90; Moderate impairment: 30 < 60; Severe impairment and kidney failure not receiving dialysis: < 30]
rules.exceptions: This classification is strictly for the purposes of conducting a dedicated renal impairment study and is not intended to be used for classifying kidney disease

rules.rule_text: Participants with impaired renal function enrolled in dedicated renal impairment studies must have stable renal function; patients with acute changes in renal function must not be included.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1 – Study Participants
rules.verbatim_quote: Participants with impaired renal function should have stable renal function (i.e., patients with acute changes in renal function should not be included in the studies discussed in this section).

rules.rule_text: Participants with impaired renal function must be similar to the control group with respect to factors known to affect the drug's PK (demographics, pharmacogenetic factors, weight), and participants taking drugs likely to impact drug metabolism or excretion must be excluded.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1 – Study Participants
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, participants with impaired renal function should be similar to the control group with respect to factors known to affect the drug's PK, for example, demographics (e.g., age, sex, race, ethnicity), pharmacogenetic factors, or weight. Participants taking drugs that are likely to impact drug metabolism or excretion should be excluded from renal impairment studies.

rules.rule_text: The number of participants enrolled in each renal function group must be sufficient to ensure a precise estimation of the relevant pharmacokinetic parameters, with justification for the sample size selected.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.2 – Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: The number of participants enrolled in each renal function group should be sufficient to ensure a precise estimation of the relevant pharmacokinetic parameters. Justification should be provided for the sample size selected. For example, one approach could be to prospectively target a 95 percent confidence interval within 60 percent and 140 percent of the geometric mean estimate of relevant pharmacokinetic parameters for the drug in each renal function group with at least 80 percent power.

rules.rule_text: Plasma or whole blood must be collected and analyzed for the parent drug and any active metabolites; collection of urine samples must be considered when the drug is primarily renally cleared.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.4 – Sample Collection and Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Plasma or whole blood should be collected and analyzed for the parent drug and any active metabolites. Collection of urine samples should be considered when the drug is primarily renally cleared.

rules.rule_text: Plasma protein binding must be assessed in renal impairment studies since it is often altered in patients with impaired renal function; measurement of unbound drug concentrations is recommended for each plasma sample only if binding is concentration-dependent or affected by metabolites or other time-varying factors.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.4 – Sample Collection and Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Plasma protein binding is often altered in patients with impaired renal function. For systemically circulating active drugs and metabolites, the unbound concentrations generally determine the rate and extent of delivery to the sites of action. Measurement of unbound drug concentrations is recommended for each plasma sample only if the binding is concentration-dependent or is affected by metabolites or other time-varying factors.
rules.exceptions: For drugs and metabolites with a low extent of plasma protein binding (e.g., less than 90 percent), description and analysis of PK in terms of total concentrations is sufficient

rules.rule_text: For a reduced pharmacokinetic study design, participants with severe renal impairment or kidney failure not on dialysis (GFR < 30 mL/min) must be enrolled to represent the worst-case scenario.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C – Reduced Pharmacokinetic Study Design
rules.verbatim_quote: The intent of a reduced pharmacokinetic study design is to represent a worst-case scenario, i.e., one that shows the greatest impact that impaired renal function could have on the PK of the drug. Prior submissions to the Agency and literature reports suggest that for such a study, participants with severe renal impairment or kidney failure not on dialysis (see Table 1) can be considered to represent the worst-case scenario.

rules.rule_text: If a reduced pharmacokinetic study shows a clinically relevant effect on PK in participants with severe renal impairment or kidney failure, the sponsor must characterize the effect in participants with other levels of impaired renal function.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C – Reduced Pharmacokinetic Study Design
rules.verbatim_quote: If a reduced pharmacokinetic study shows a clinically relevant effect on the PK of the drug in participants with severe renal impairment or kidney failure not on dialysis, the sponsor should characterize the effect of impaired renal function on the drug's PK in participants with the other levels of impaired renal function (see Table 1). If no difference in PK is observed between participants with severe renal impairment or kidney failure not on dialysis compared to normal renal function, then no further study is recommended.
rules.exceptions: No further study needed if no PK difference observed between severe impairment and normal function

rules.rule_text: When characterizing the impact of renal function using phase 2 and phase 3 data via population PK analysis, renal function should be assessed as an independent predictor of exposure-response relationships.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D – Characterizing the Impact of Renal Function in Phase 2 and Phase 3 Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: When possible, renal function should be assessed as an independent predictor of exposure-response relationships.

rules.rule_text: For popPK approaches using phase 2/3 data, the study design must retain: sufficient numbers of participants over a wide range of renal function, accurate records of dosing and sample collection times, adequate numbers of samples per participant, unbound drug concentrations when appropriate, and active metabolite levels.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D – Characterizing the Impact of Renal Function in Phase 2 and Phase 3 Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: The following are important considerations: Sufficient numbers of participants over a wide range of renal function; Accurate records of dosing and sample collection times; Adequate numbers of samples per participant; Unbound drug concentrations when appropriate; Active metabolite levels, where applicable, in addition to levels of the parent drug; Use of the same equation to estimate renal function, especially when data are pooled across studies; Exposure-response analyses, when available, should account for impaired renal function as an independent predictor of response.

rules.rule_text: To evaluate the impact of dialysis on drug PK, studies must be conducted in patients undergoing intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) using high-flux dialyzers, as IHD is currently the most common dialysis modality in the United States.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.4 / Section IV.E.1 – Intermittent Renal Replacement Therapies
rules.verbatim_quote: Intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) is currently the most common dialysis modality used in patients being treated with renal replacement therapy in the United States. Therefore, to evaluate the impact of dialysis on the PK of a drug, we recommend that sponsors conduct these studies in patients undergoing IHD... Because most dialysis centers in the United States currently use high-flux dialyzers during IHD, pharmacokinetic studies should be conducted in patients being treated with high-flux IHD.
rules.exceptions: Similar principles can be applied to characterize the impact of peritoneal dialysis or CRRT

rules.rule_text: IHD studies must include both on- and off-dialysis periods; each participant must receive a single dose on two occasions — once administered prior to a dialysis session and once administered to reflect exposures expected during an off-dialysis day.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.1 – Intermittent Renal Replacement Therapies
rules.verbatim_quote: It is critical for the IHD study to include both on- and off-dialysis periods. Each participant should receive a single dose on two occasions, once with the dose administered prior to a dialysis session, with dialysis typically commencing just before the anticipated time to maximum concentration (Tmax) following administration of the drug. On the second occasion, the drug should be administered in such a way that it reflects the exposures expected during an off-dialysis day.

rules.rule_text: IHD studies must record blood flow (QB), dialysate flow (QD), ultrafiltration clearance (QUF), and the make and model of the dialyzer to interpret study results and extrapolate to other dialysis conditions.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.1 – Intermittent Renal Replacement Therapies
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important to record the blood flow (QB), the dialysate flow (QD), ultrafiltration clearance (QUF), and the make and model of the dialyzer used in the study to interpret study results and extrapolate to other dialysis conditions.

rules.rule_text: For CRRT studies, the entire dialysate must be collected, its volume recorded, and a sample retained to determine drug concentrations; simultaneous pre-filter, post-filter, and effluent samples for drug and active metabolites must be collected.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.3 – Sample Collection and Data Analysis (IHD) / Section IV.E.3.b (CRRT)
rules.verbatim_quote: The entire dialysate should be collected, its volume recorded, and a sample retained to determine drug concentrations... In addition to collecting simultaneous pre-filter, post-filter, and effluent samples for drug and active metabolites, additional time-matching data measuring CRRT therapy intensity (e.g., blood flow rate, replacement fluid flow rate, ultrafiltration rate, dialysis flow rate) should be captured to determine extracorporeal clearance (CLEC).

rules.rule_text: Blood samples before and at the end of dialysis must also be used to measure drug binding to plasma proteins; the fraction of the administered dose recovered in the dialysate must be calculated to assess the need for supplemental drug doses to hemodialysis patients.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.3.a – Sample Collection and Data Analysis: IHD
rules.verbatim_quote: Blood samples before and at the end of dialysis should also be used to measure drug binding to plasma proteins. The fraction of the administered dose that is recovered in the dialysate should be calculated to assess the need for administering supplemental drug doses to hemodialysis patients.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacodynamic assessments must be included in renal impairment studies whenever appropriate and feasible, particularly in situations where impaired renal function can result in pharmacodynamic changes independent of pharmacokinetic changes.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F – Considerations for Pharmacodynamic Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever appropriate and feasible, pharmacodynamic assessments should be included in renal impairment studies. Such assessments are important in situations where impaired renal function can result in pharmacodynamic changes that are independent of pharmacokinetic changes (e.g., oral anticoagulants). In such situations, the pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic data could be critical to determine the appropriate recommended dosage in patients with impaired renal function.
rules.exceptions: The selection of PD endpoints should be discussed with the relevant review division

rules.rule_text: Plasma concentration data and, where available, urinary excretion data must be analyzed to estimate PK parameters of the drug and its active metabolites, including AUC, Cmax, fraction unbound (fu), apparent clearance (CL/F), renal clearance (CLR), apparent nonrenal clearance (CLNR/F), apparent volume of distribution (V/F), and effective and terminal half-life.
rules.source_section: Section V.A – Estimating Pharmacokinetic Parameters
rules.verbatim_quote: Plasma concentration data and, where available, urinary excretion data should be analyzed to estimate pharmacokinetic parameters of the drug and its active metabolites. The pharmacokinetic parameters of interest include the area under the plasma concentration-time curve (AUC), peak concentration (Cmax), fraction unbound (fu), apparent clearance (CL/F), renal clearance (CLR), apparent nonrenal clearance (CLNR/F), apparent volume of distribution (V/F), and effective and terminal half-life (t1/2), where applicable.

rules.rule_text: The FDA recommends a regression approach in which estimated individualized renal function and PK parameters are treated as continuous variables for modeling the relationship between renal function and PK parameters.
rules.source_section: Section V.B – Modeling the Relationship Between Renal Function and Pharmacokinetic Parameters
rules.verbatim_quote: The FDA recommends a regression approach in which estimated individualized renal function and the pharmacokinetic parameters are treated as continuous variables. This method is usually preferred to an analysis in which estimated renal function is treated as a categorical variable... because it allows the identification of thresholds that could be more meaningful for derivation of dosage adjustments.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must calculate and report estimates of the parameters of the chosen model as well as measures of their precision (e.g., standard errors or confidence intervals).
rules.source_section: Section V.B – Modeling the Relationship Between Renal Function and Pharmacokinetic Parameters
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should calculate and report estimates of the parameters of the chosen model as well as measures of their precision (e.g., standard errors or confidence intervals).

rules.rule_text: Dosage recommendations in patients with impaired renal function must be determined based on the overall understanding of the relationship between renal function, drug exposure, and exposure-response relationships (efficacy and safety).
rules.source_section: Section V.C – Developing Dosage Recommendations in Patients with Impaired Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: Dosage recommendations in patients with impaired renal function should be determined based on the overall understanding of the relationship between renal function, drug exposure, and the exposure-response relationships (efficacy and safety).
rules.exceptions: For drugs with a wide therapeutic range, changes in the drug's PK based on renal function might not always result in a different recommended dosage

rules.rule_text: When a different dosage recommendation for patients with impaired renal function is needed, dosage recommendations must be based on exposure-matching to a reference group with an acceptable benefit-risk profile; the reference group does not have to be limited to the normal renal function group.
rules.source_section: Section V.C – Developing Dosage Recommendations in Patients with Impaired Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: When there is a need for a different dosage recommendation for patients with impaired renal function, these dosage recommendations should be based on exposure-matching to a reference group with an acceptable benefit-risk profile for the drug. Hence, the reference group does not have to be limited to the normal renal function group.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must use modeling and simulation data to determine the level of renal function for which a different dosage is recommended and are not required to rely on pre-specified categories of impaired renal function.
rules.source_section: Section V.C – Developing Dosage Recommendations in Patients with Impaired Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should use the data from modeling and simulation to determine the level of renal function for which a different dosage is recommended. It is not always necessary to rely on pre-specified categories of impaired renal function as shown in Table 1.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must evaluate the impact of different contemporary estimating equations of renal function on drug dosing recommendations and document the reasons for any discrepant results.
rules.source_section: Section V.C – Developing Dosage Recommendations in Patients with Impaired Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: In such situations, dosage recommendations in patients with impaired renal function can significantly differ if different estimating equations of renal function are used. Hence, as a good practice, it is important to evaluate the impact of different contemporary estimating equations of renal function on drug dosing recommendations. In such situations, it is important to understand the reasons underlying the discrepant results.

rules.rule_text: For CRRT dosing recommendations, the basis must be the intensity of the CRRT regimen as CLEC is influenced by prescribed blood, dialysis, and ultrafiltration flow rates; different recommended dosages may be needed for different filter types and CRRT modalities.
rules.source_section: Section V.C – Developing Dosage Recommendations in Patients with Impaired Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: In CRRT, dosing recommendations should be based on the intensity of the CRRT regimen, as CLEC is known to be influenced by the prescribed blood, dialysis, and ultrafiltration flow rates... There may be different recommended dosages for different filter types and CRRT modalities. The sponsor should provide data/justification to inform the dosage recommendations for other CRRT modalities.

rules.rule_text: The Prescribing Information (labeling) must include a summary of essential information for safe and effective use in patients with impaired renal function, including: PK and PD in patients with impaired renal function including those receiving dialysis, information on renal elimination, clinical effects of PK/PD changes, and dosage or monitoring recommendations.
rules.source_section: Section VI – Labeling Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: The Prescribing Information should include a summary of essential information needed for the safe and effective use of the drug in patients with impaired renal function, as appropriate, and should include the following, as appropriate: PK and PD in patients with impaired renal function, including those receiving dialysis; Information on renal elimination of the drug and relevant active metabolites; Clinical effects of pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic changes in patients with impaired renal function; Recommendations to prevent, mitigate, monitor for, or manage risks in patients with impaired renal function (e.g., different dosage or monitoring recommendations).

rules.rule_text: For pharmacokinetic studies, estimation of renal function using eGFR (expressed as mL/minute, not referenced to 1.73 m2 BSA) using a contemporary, widely accepted equation is recommended over eCLcr; eGFR in mL/minute must be calculated by multiplying eGFR referenced to BSA of 1.73 m2 with the individual's BSA and dividing by 1.73.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A – Determination of Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: Because of the widespread availability and incorporation of eGFR into current clinical practice, use of eGFR to determine renal function in PK studies is recommended over eCLcr... when providing a recommended dosage in adult patients with renal impairment, use the eGFR expressed as mL/minute rather than the eGFR referenced to a BSA value of 1.73 m2. To compute eGFR in mL/minute, multiply the eGFR referenced to a BSA of 1.73 m2 with the individual's BSA (calculated using an appropriate formula) and divide by 1.73.
rules.exceptions: Use of measured GFR using an exogenous marker, measured CLcr, or any contemporary, widely accepted, and clinically applicable estimating equation is also considered reasonable

rules.rule_text: In overweight or obese individuals, use of alternative body weight metrics such as ideal body weight (IBW) or adjusted body weight (ABW) when calculating eCLcr using the Cockcroft-Gault equation is likely to provide a more accurate estimate of renal function than using actual body weight.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A – Determination of Renal Function
rules.verbatim_quote: In overweight or obese individuals, use of alternative body weight metrics such as ideal body weight (IBW) or adjusted body weight (ABW) when calculating eCLcr using the C-G equation is likely to provide a more accurate estimate of renal function than using actual body weight.

rules.rule_text: Study participants enrolled in dedicated renal impairment studies must be enrolled based on one common estimation method to determine renal function in control and renal impairment groups.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1 – Study Participants / Table 1
rules.verbatim_quote: Study participants should be enrolled based on one common estimation method to determine renal function in control and renal impairment groups.

rules.rule_text: For assessing the antibody-drug conjugate impact of renal impairment, assessment of renal impairment on ADC PK is important and must be considered.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.3 – Certain Therapeutic Proteins and Peptides
rules.verbatim_quote: Assessing the effect of renal impairment on the PK of antibody-drug conjugates is also important.
cross_references: FDA guidance: Clinical Pharmacology Considerations for Antibody-Drug Conjugates (March 2024)

rules.rule_text: For CRRT studies, sponsors are strongly encouraged to seek FDA's input early in the process of designing such studies due to the practical challenges and wide variability of CRRT methods across institutions.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.2 – Continuous Renal Replacement Therapies
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors are strongly encouraged to seek FDA's input early in the process of designing such studies.

rules.rule_text: The potential for rebound due to redistribution of drug or active metabolites from peripheral tissues after dialysis must be analyzed and its implications for dosage recommendations must be characterized.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E.3.a – Sample Collection and Data Analysis: IHD
rules.verbatim_quote: The analysis should also consider the potential for a rebound due to redistribution of drug or active metabolites from peripheral tissues after dialysis and characterize its implications for dosage recommendations.
